answer:
verdict: ur moat was never the model, it was always gonna be everything around it. 6mo tech lead = 0 moat, so stop defending the feature & start building switching costs

where defensibility actually comes from now:

1. **workflow depth** — embed so deep in customer ops that ripping u out = painful migration. integrations, permissions, audit logs, admin tooling. boring stuff APIs dont give u
2. **proprietary data loop** — if usage generates data that improves ur product 4 ur customers specifically (fine-tunes, evals, domain feedback), copycats cant replicate. key q: do u actually have this or just usage logs? be honest
3. **distribution + brand** — 18mo head start shld = customers, case studies, SEO, sales motion. commoditized tech + owned channel still wins (see: most SaaS)
4. **vertical specialization** — GPT-tier API is generic. domain-specific UX, compliance (SOC2/HIPAA if relevant), edge cases, templates, onboarding = stuff horizontal players wont bother w/
5. **multi-model arbitrage** — flip the threat: commoditized models = ur COGS drop too. route to cheapest/best model per task, compete on price + reliability while wrappers eat api costs raw
6. **speed as culture not lead** — 6mo ahead once means nothing. shipping 2x faster forever means a lot

what NOT to do: pour more $ into rebuilding the core feature to stay ahead of foundation labs. u will lose that race, they spend billions

practical next 90 days: talk to top 10 customers, ask "what wld make leaving us painful" — build that. + audit churn risk: anyone using u as thin layer over the AI feature will leave 1st, anyone using workflows/integrations stays

real talk: if product = thin wrapper w/ no data loop, no workflow depth, no distribution edge, then answer is pivot into 1 of those fast, not defend. 6mo is enough runway to do it if u start now
